version: EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) — curated tiering rules

prohibited.description: Article 5 — practices banned outright in the EU.
obligations: Do not place the system on the market or put it into service in the EU.
Discontinue any existing equivalent use immediately.
triggers: social scoring of natural persons by public authorities
predictive policing based solely on profiling of individuals
real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions)
subliminal manipulation causing significant harm
exploitation of vulnerabilities of specific groups
untargeted scraping of facial images for facial-recognition databases
emotion recognition in workplaces and educational institutions (with narrow exceptions)

high_risk.description: Annex III high-risk systems and safety components of regulated products. Subject to the full conformity regime: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record-keeping, transparency, human oversight, accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity, post-market monitoring, registration.
obligations: Establish and maintain a risk management system across the lifecycle (Art. 9).
Use training/validation/test data that meets quality criteria (Art. 10).
Maintain technical documentation and event logs (Art. 11, 12).
Meet accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity requirements (Art. 15).
Register the system in the EU database (Art. 49).
Conduct a fundamental-rights impact assessment if the deployer is a public authority (Art. 27).
triggers: biometric identification or categorization of natural persons
management or operation of critical infrastructure (energy, water, transport)
education and vocational training (admission, evaluation, proctoring)
employment, worker management, access to self-employment (recruiting, evaluating, terminating)
access to essential private and public services and benefits
law enforcement (risk assessment, evidence reliability, profiling)
migration, asylum, border control
administration of justice and democratic processes
safety component of regulated products under EU sectoral law

obligations: Inform users they are interacting with an AI system when not obvious.
Disclose use of emotion-recognition or biometric-categorization systems to affected persons.
triggers: chatbot or conversational interface with humans
emotion recognition (outside prohibited contexts)
biometric categorization (outside prohibited contexts)
generated or manipulated image, audio, or video (deepfake)

minimal_risk.description: No specific obligations under the AI Act beyond existing law. Voluntary codes of conduct encouraged.
obligations: Existing horizontal law (GDPR, product safety, sectoral rules) still applies.
Voluntary adoption of a code of conduct is encouraged (Art. 95).
triggers: spam filter
AI-enabled video game
inventory optimization
generic productivity tooling without rights-affecting decisions
